Alan Freed was a disc jockey at radio station WJW in 1951. As he played uptempo Black rhythm and blues records on his show, he started referring to them with a three-word phrase. This became the name of a genre that came to define American music for the rest of the century. What term did Freed coin?
I have two guesses for the two parts of question 😂

First part - Jazz and Blues.
Last line - Rock and roll
rock and roll is the correct answer
Jazz and Blues was a forced answer 🫢

1. Okay, starting with some from   India and  general vicinity :
Collectively, these flags denote a certain entity and were used to represent it (e.g. at olympics etc). Which entity?
Brownie points if you can ID each flag.
(Easy one to begin with, because it has a motif that’s all too familiar to us 🤣) ‎image omitted
that’s the star of India, the flag of British Raj
Yes. Both are British India flags.
Can anyone ID the specific flags?
the first is the EIC flag until 1857 and the second is under crown rule?
Speaking of, there was scene in the movie Sinners depicting Black rhythm through the ages and it was a visual masterpiece
Nope, both flags are for under crown rule.
Was the first until 1947, and the second between 1947-1950?
ok then the first one is the flag of the Raj, second one is the Viceroy’s flag?
both are prior to Independence. (It’s a bit esoteric tbf)
oh wow, i was about to say, or the other way around 🙂
The first is the British Indian Ensign. The bottom is the Governor General’s Standard, as indicated by the Crown.
the British have this concept of a monarch’s flag that’s separate from the coutnry’s flag, they fly it on Windsor when the king is in
Nice crack! Would have never guessed that way. My guess was gonn be first was flag used by British themselves and second was the flag to be used by princely states under British rule.
The big clue ofc is the Star of India as @⁨~Pavan Pamidimarri⁩  mentioned, which is something we all see way too much of 🤣
yeah i have seen the flag of Hyderabad
can’t be a viceroy, was a governor general

I was scrolling down thinking no one answered it :) anyway Karmarkar's idea and algorithm are quite brilliant, first to establish an end to end to polynomial time solution, however the idea of interior point methods started in Russia in the 60s. After Karmarkar in the 90s two Russian mathematicians working in the area of optimization took this idea much further along allowing for interior point methods to work for convex constraint set and not just polytopes. Both have names starting with N. Can we name them?
These mathematicians have influenced the growth of AI significantly as well, making fundamental contributions to the optimization algorithms that are now used to pre-train AI models. ‎<This message was edited>
I was interested in the backlash from other computer scientists that algorithms should not be late yes and even the US supreme Court precedent that you cannot patent mathematical formulae

Oh I didn't mean to say there was some sort of 'stealing' or anything at all .. Karmarkar afaiu brought a program to it's first concrete provable result and it remains a huge achievement. The Russia thing was a segue to my eventual question :) ‎<This message was edited>

Easy one (as I’m in a train journey lined with British cathedrals)
In Britain, medieval cathedrals often come in two striking “styles”: some reach for the heavens with spires, while others have broad, flat-topped towers.
Take Salisbury Cathedral, whose soaring spire remains the tallest in the UK, compared with York Minster, whose towers are famously flat-topped and fortress-like.
So, why do we see this mix of spired and flat-topped cathedrals?
Yes basically. Making solid and structurally safe spires  (after some incidents) required money and many places weren’t funded for it because they weren’t quite “as important”
Nice! I couldn’t remember the other author. I remember seeing their monograph on interior pt methods in my advisor’s office

Yup although their greater contribution to our society would be their work on optimization algorithms like gradient descent and a lot of advancements on it which forms one of the bedrock of LLM pretraining today
Nestorov has the fancy gradient thingy no, in which future estimates of the gradient are used at the current pt?
In modern parlance just shortened to Nesterov
I vaguely remember, never used though
So Nesterov's advisor Boris Polyak had the core kernel of the idea, called Polyak's heavy ball momentum
^this has very neat physical interpretations as the name suggests
Nesterov's work is more of a tour de force through math to come up with a related method that provably works in all scenarios ‎<This message was edited>
But 30 years later people were still peeling that onion, to come up with alternative derivations that might be easier to digest and understand

I mean these are fundamentally tweaks to the gradient no? Whether we somehow blend past values and/or future estimates, esp for large optimization problems. In many cases they do have nice physical analogies, and even interpretations..
I am not even close to an expert lol
Never thought gradient descent and Gafoor 🎶 would appear together in a discussion 😆
Yes absolutely, but the fun part is that they make quite a significant difference to the base method
And they have connections to even things like damped harmonic oscillators
And fundamental results on approximations via polynomials
It's quite fun to see the same 'mathematical' phenomenon appear across different unrelated settings
Anyway this is getting into spam territory now :)
It isn’t spam for me ;) i learn a lot

A girl in a YouTube video said that life in the 4 big American cities is different from life in other places in American. Of course, the comment section was fun because she did not mention which 4 cities and nobody could agree.

Chicago would be pretty high then

Maybe a tough fight with LA for second

there is no pop culture without LA and NY

X and Y decided to use the changing color schemes to enhance the narrative, using different color palettes to mark the different eras of the protagonist's life. This fit perfectly into the film, Z, which was based on the memoirs of a well-known Marathi stage and screen actress, Hansa Wadkar, whose career lasted decades in the industry.

I was about to give another piece of trivia from the film which I came across recently. For one of the scenes, Amrish Puri was supposed to slap Smita Patil. Unbeknownst to the actress, Amrish Puri and Shyam Benegal decided that Amrish will slap her for real during the shot to get a natural reaction.

Here's an extract from Amrish Puri's autobiography recounting this incident: "Normally, we slap in such a way that without hitting the other actor, you have to react. I discussed with Shyam that by giving a real slap we would get the right reaction. He approved of the idea but warned me not to slap her forcefully. I rehearsed the scene without hitting her but slapped her in the final shot not realising the impact. It had the desired effect and what a fantastic reaction came on her face, as she looked daggers at me. It conveyed, “How dare you slap me?” But Smita was a natural actress and very professional. Her performance was always acclaimed. As the shot ended she ran after me to hit me. The whole unit was enjoying the incident."

7/10: A Telugu word for “a typesetting error made during printing” is the name of a famous Sanskrit play. The play tells the story of the rise to power of Chandragupta Maurya and served as a source for the TV series Chanakya broadcast in the 1990s. The play was written several centuries after the events described, so it blends historical events with creative storytelling. It is notable for the absence of a romance track, and it lacks a prominent leading female character. The Telugu use for “typo” is a pun on the play’s name. Name the play.

I have a few questions about what foreign travelers wrote about India in the past. Should I post them now?

Marco Polo, a Venetian merchant, visited India on his way back home from China to Venice around 1290 CE. He arrived by ship from Southeast Asia and spent time in the city of X located in the delta of the Y river. For several centuries before Polo’s arrival, X was the capital of the Chola kingdom, but their long-time rivals, the Z dynasty, annexed the city a couple of decades before Polo got there. Polo, who was aware of most of Eurasia at the time, described this region as “the richest and most splendid province in the world”. Identify X, Y, and Z.
And Pandya for the rival dynasty?

Polo commented on the skin color preferences of people in Thanjavur. He said that a certain skin color is highly esteemed among these people. “When a child is born, they anoint him once a week with the oil of sesame, as it makes the skin much ____. No wonder all their gods are ____ and all their devils are _____ as ____.” FITB.
I am going to say former
what skin color did they prefer
what were their gods like and what color were their devils?
your original answer had the key terms, but need to pick which side is which
And fair for demons. As in ravana
Polo says that the people of south India used to prefer dark skin color
they used to apply sesame oil to make the skin much darker
so he says no wonder their gods are dark and their devils white as snow
the colorism we see in India did not exist back then, if we believe Polo
at least not in the south
Seems to be a medieval fixation
Because traditionally many gods are depicted as dark
Fair and Lovely would have no market in 13th century India

Polo described a visit northeast from Thanjavur to the tomb of one of the twelve apostles of Jesus Christ. He mentioned that the place was a pilgrimage site for both Christians and Muslims. A basilica was built at the site centuries later under Portuguese patronage, and centuries later still, a city was founded nearby which now contains this basilica. Name the apostle and the city.
At that time it was called Mylapore, now of course part of Chennai
Named after the restaurant chain in bay area
Polo refers to it as Ma’bar or something like that

I am not sold on this one. The prosody + learning by rote mechanisms are fantastic innovations to protect the structural integrity of the verse over many many generations, but it doesn't promote critical understanding of the meaning. Once writing was invented, it was possible to transmit knowledge well without that much effort. But Indian culture has always had a strong reverence for the old ways.
It is interesting to read al-Biruni's critique of the Indians' methods
It might not promote critical understanding by itself but it’s an important starting point.

So this is my two cents from my time as an art student and teacher - one thing that did categorically stay a bit underwhelming in India compared to the West was the development of visual art and techniques through 15-18th century. The pure invention of brush techniques such as sfumato, chiaroscuro, pointillism, impressionism, etc.l was revolutionary in the way art reflected on contemporary society. We did have a surge of art schools 18-19th century onwards (think Kalighat or Kangra), and a major focus leant on architecture and textile/surface art, but it always bums me that we did not have that rich a legacy of art as in other fields

Durga, a 16 year old kid, used to sing in trains in suburban Mumbai and try to make two meals for her two sisters. She was spotted by a film director, who loved the rustic texture of her voice and recommended her to his music director friend. The music director trained the girl on the spot, made her record a track over 4 days, which became a huge hit. Not just this, the music director scouted kids from an extremely backward rat-eating community in Gangetic plains and made them record another fabulous song in the same movie. This is my question using the same image... Kharosthi is the name of the earliest script used on the Indian subcontinent. It is dated to circa the third century BCE. It was used on coins and inscriptions to write Sanskrit, Pali, and the local dialects of the region. Below is a picture of a paper strip with Kharosthi writing on it. The script was deciphered by James Princep in the early 19th century by studying coins with both Indian and Greek lettering on them. While Kharosthi was an important step in the adoption of a written alphabet in India, it differs from all Indic scripts in one crucial respect. What is the difference?

The literal meaning of the name of this place is “abode of the deathless.” The name of this real place is also used as the name for heaven in Hindu mythology. This small riverside town today was once the capital of a mighty empire. Many centuries ago, it was home to what was likely the largest Buddhist stupa in the Indian subcontinent. Buddhist chronicles in Tibet and Sri Lanka mention the huge stupa at this place, and the Dalai Lama performed a ceremony here in 2006. After the decline of Buddhism in India, the place was buried under rubble and was rediscovered in the 19th century. Name this town and the state it is in.
On the banks of the Krishna river

last question for a bit… The national anthem of this country has verses in five different languages. The first two lines are from a church hymn composed by a teacher at a Methodist mission school. That song got translated into other languages and became the national anthem of four other countries. Name the country with the five-language national anthem that shifts and ends in a different key from the one it began in.

South Africa. . Its national anthem starts with Nkosi Sikelele iAfrika, a Xhosa hymn that means "God bless Africa." In translation, it was adopted as the national anthem by Zimbabwe, Zambia, Tanzania and Namibia. In the apartheid years, the hymn Nkosi Sikelele became a symbol of protest against the regime. When Mandela became president, it acquired joint status as one of the two national anthems, alongside the old apartheid anthem Die Stem van Suid Afrika ("The Call of South Africa"). Later, a single anthem was created incorporating verses in Xhosa, the Zulu translation of Nkosi Sikelele, Sotho, Afrikaans and English. The original hymn in translation is still the anthem of Zambia and Tanzania. Zimbabwe and Namibia have since changed their anthems.

Vivek Express is the name of multiple trains in India, named after Swami Vivekananda. The Frontier Mail was an important express train that carried passengers and mail across British India. It ran from Bombay to Peshawar and The Times of London described it as “one of the most famous express trains within the British Empire.” This train notched up many firsts, including the first air-conditioned cars in India and the first radio service on an Indian train. What is the current name of this train, and what points does it run between?
Studied in vellore and used to travel through kerela express
Golden Temple Mail, between Bandra Terminus, Maharashtra and Amritsar, Punjab
After Amritsar the train used to go to Lahore. that route now is traversed, in good times, by the Samjhauta Express
Which was protested by Maj Raghavan in “Main Hoon Na” 🤣🤣🤣

X is the oldest running train in the Indian railway network. It was the principal mechanism by which the British Raj shifted its capital from X to Y every year and back. When it began, the train numbers were 01 and 02, and it ran as the East Indian Railway Mail. It lost its premier numbering in the 1990s and is now numbered 12311/12312. Satyajit Ray wrote one of his Feluda mysteries on this train. ID X and the termini of this train.

candidates.text: Indian railways has been the backbone of transportation, commerce, and communication that binds our country together. It is also a custodian of our heritage. Indian railways operates a UNESCO World Heritage Site comprised of three lines collectively titled the “Mountain Railways of India.” The first to receive this designation is in the east of India, a two-foot gauge line built around 1880 and around 88 km in length. It received this status in 1999. Where is this line?

The second railway line to receive UNESCO World Heritage Site status is in the south of India and received this status in 2005. The railway opened in 1899 with a meter-gauge, and it is the only rack-and-pinion railway in India. Steam locomotives can traverse the entire 46 km line, while diesel locomotives are used on a partial section. Where is this railway?

A probe landed in 2005 very close to Y, at a place named after Melanasian mythological paradise. This probe was named after the person who had discovered the place Z where Y and the other site are located.
Now comes the most interesting bit. You could wonder why X is named so, and the answer lies in its design. When you see the design, it reminds you of a movie P, that had very similar design elements in its props. And when you go into a proverbial rabbit hole, you realize that several names from movie P or its original text are directly referenced to name different things on Z.
